10: The judgment seat of Christ (bema tou Christou, 2 Corinthians 5:10): the bema was the elevated platform in a Roman city center where the magistrate or proconsul rendered judgments — Gallio sat on the Corinthian bema when Jews dragged Paul before him (Acts 18:12). Paul transforms this civic institution into an eschatological reality: each person will appear before Christ's bema to receive what is due for what was done in the body, whether good or evil. This is not justification-reversal (Paul consistently teaches no condemnation for those in Christ, Romans 8:1) but an accounting of stewardship — the believer's life-work is evaluated by the risen judge.
1: For we know that if the tent (skēnos) that is our earthly home is destroyed, we have a building from God, a house not made with hands (oikodomēn ek theou), eternal in the heavens — the tent as body metaphor was used in Greek philosophy (Plato, Phaedo 81c: the soul is dragged back into the visible by the body as a tent peg). Paul transforms the image: the tent is not an evil prison but a temporary dwelling whose replacement is not dissolution but transformation.
2: For in this tent we groan, longing to put on our heavenly dwelling (to oikētērion hēmōn to ex ouranou ependysasthai) — the clothing metaphor for the resurrection body (1 Corinthians 15:53-54) reappears. Ependysasthai (to put on over, to clothe oneself additionally) implies transformation rather than replacement: the new body is put on over the old, not in place of it.
3: If indeed by putting it on we may not be found naked — the nakedness of a disembodied state was feared in Greek thought and Jewish apocalyptic. Paul's point is that the resurrection body prevents the nakedness of mere soul-without-body existence.
4: For while we are still in this tent, we groan, being burdened — not that we would be unclothed, but that we would be further clothed, so that what is mortal may be swallowed up by life — the Epicurean and Platonic traditions both envisioned death as a release from bodily burden; Paul's vision is the opposite: not escape from the body but the transformation of mortality into immortality.
5: He who has prepared us for this very thing is God, who has given us the Spirit as a guarantee (arrabōn) — the arrabōn (down-payment, earnest money) reappears from 2 Corinthians 1:22. The commercial metaphor was familiar from Hellenistic papyri; the Spirit is God's advance payment guaranteeing the full transformation to come.
6: So we are always of good courage (tharrountes) — tharreō (to be courageous, take heart) is Paul's word throughout chapters 5 and 7 for the apostolic confidence grounded in resurrection hope. We know that while we are at home (endemountes) in the body we are away from home (ekdemountes) from the Lord.
7: For we walk by faith, not by sight (dia pisteos peripatoumen, ou dia eidous) — the contrast between pistis (faith) and eidos (sight, appearance) reflects the epistemological framework Paul has been developing since 2 Corinthians 4:18 (visible/invisible). Walking dia pisteos rather than dia eidous is the apostolic mode of navigation through the world.
8: Yes, we are of good courage, and we would rather be away from the body and at home with the Lord — the preference for being with the Lord rather than in the body mirrors Philippians 1:21-23 (to live is Christ, to die is gain; I desire to depart and be with Christ). Both passages reflect a genuinely positive assessment of death for the believer, though resurrection remains the final goal.
9: So whether we are at home or away, we make it our aim to please him — the ambition (philotimoumetha, to love honor, to be ambitious) Paul redirects from self-advancement to pleasing God. The word philotimia in Roman culture described the honor-seeking of the elite; Paul converts it into service-orientation.
11: Therefore, knowing the fear of the Lord, we persuade others — the fear of God (phobos tou kyriou) as the motivation for persuasion (peithomen) reflects the prophetic tradition where awareness of divine judgment drives the proclamation. The same awareness of standing before God governs 4:2 and 2 Corinthians 2:17.
12: We are not commending ourselves to you again but giving you cause to boast about us — the letter is not another self-defense (apologeomai) but provision for the Corinthians' own boasting (kauchēma) on Paul's behalf. Those who boast in outward appearance (en prosōpō) rather than the heart reflect the super-apostles' appeal to visible credentials.
13: For if we are beside ourselves (exestēmen, we have been out of our minds), it is for God; if we are in our right mind (sōphronoumen), it is for you — the alleged madness of Paul's ecstatic experiences (mystical visions, speaking in tongues) serves God; his sober self-presentation serves the congregation. The tension mirrors 1 Corinthians 14:18-19: Paul speaks in tongues more than all but prefers five intelligible words in the assembly.
14: For the love of Christ controls us (synechei hēmas, holds us together, constrains us) — synechō (to press together, hold in place) was used for armies hemming in an enemy and for the grip of disease. The love of Christ is Paul's controlling force; it presses him into service rather than allowing drift.
15: And he died for all, that those who live might no longer live for themselves but for him who for their sake died and was raised — the died-for-all (hyper pantōn) and the living-no-longer-for-self maps the atonement's ethical consequence. The purpose clause (hina — so that) makes the death consequential: the cross creates a new mode of living.
16: From now on, therefore, we regard no one according to the flesh (kata sarka) — the eschatological dividing line (from now on, apo tou nyn) marks the new-creation epistemology: the old categories of human evaluation no longer apply. Even though we once regarded Christ according to the flesh, we regard him thus no longer — Paul's pre-conversion understanding of Jesus as a failed messianic pretender has been overturned by the resurrection.
17: Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation (kainē ktisis) — the phrase kainē ktisis (new creation) appears in Galatians 6:15 as the thing that counts beyond circumcision or uncircumcision. Jewish apocalyptic awaited a new creation (1 Enoch 2 Corinthians 45:4-5; 2 Corinthians 72:1; Isaiah 65:17); Paul announces that it has begun in Christ.
18: All this is from God, who through Christ reconciled us to himself and gave us the ministry of reconciliation (diakonian tēs katallagēs) — katallagē (reconciliation, exchange, restoration of relationship) appears for the first time in the NT in Romans 5:10-11 and here. The word was used for the ending of hostility between parties and the restoration of commercial or diplomatic relations.
19: That is, in Christ God was reconciling the world (kosmon) to himself, not counting their trespasses against them — the cosmic scope (kosmos) of reconciliation echoes Colossians 1:20 (reconciling all things). The not-counting of trespasses (mē logizomenos, not reckoning) reflects the accounting metaphor: the debt has been cancelled.
20: Therefore, we are ambassadors for Christ (hyper Christou oun presbeuo), God making his appeal through us — presbeuo was the technical term for serving as a diplomatic envoy of a king or emperor. The ambassador speaks with the authority of the one who sent him; Paul's proclamation carries the authority of the divine King whose message he carries.
21: For our sake he made him to be sin (hamartian epoiēsen) who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God — the great exchange: Christ takes what is ours (sin) so that we receive what is his (righteousness). The phrase made him to be sin reflects both the sin-offering (hatta't) of Leviticus 4-5 where the sacrifice becomes identified with sin, and the scapegoat of Leviticus 16 that bears the community's sins away.
